explanation: The input states that when the user stops the ventilation in PCV mode, there must be a transition from PCV mode to VentilationOff (i.e., StandbyMode is entered). This implies that whenever both PCVMode is active and stopVentilation is triggered, the outcome/requirement is StandbyMode being reached (i.e., system is in Standby Mode, which is described as 'VentilationOff Mode'). The template '(G ((bool_exp1 & bool_exp2) -> bool_exp3))' is appropriate here, where bool_exp1 represents the system being in PCVMode, bool_exp2 is the user request to stop ventilation (stopVentilation), and bool_exp3 is the system being in StandbyMode (VentilationOff). This template universally states: whenever both PCVMode is active and the user triggers stopVentilation, StandbyMode must follow (i.e., the system reaches the VentilationOff/Standby state immediately after).

The property requires that if the system is currently in PCV mode and the user issues a stop ventilation command (stopVentilation), then in the next step, the system will no longer be in the PCV mode (i.e., transition out of PCV). The use of 'PCVMode & stopVentilation' as the trigger and 'X (! PCVMode)' as the effect ensures that stopping ventilation in PCV mode results in the system transitioning away from the PCV mode (i.e., to VentilationOff, as described), on the next time-step. This captures the requirement that stopping ventilation in PCV mode causes a transition out of PCV.

The given requirement states that a transition from PCV (Pressure Controlled Ventilation) mode to VentilationOff (Standby) should occur if the user stops the ventilation while the system is in PCV mode. In terms of the provided atomic propositions, 'PCVMode' indicates that the system is currently in PCV mode, and 'stopVentilation' indicates the user has stopped ventilation. To capture this causality (if the system is in PCV mode and the user stops ventilation, then a transition to VentilationOff must occur or be enabled), we express: globally, if PCVMode is active and stopVentilation occurs, then (at next instant) 'StandbyMode' (which, by description, is the same as VentilationOff) must become true. This matches template 41, which encodes this as a transition requirement on a conjunction of 'PCVMode' and 'stopVentilation' leading to the next state being 'StandbyMode'. N_DURATION is not needed for this template.

This property formalizes that, whenever the system is in PCV mode and the user issues a stop ventilation command (stopVentilation), then a transition to ventilation off (VentilationOff) should occur in the next step. Here, PCVMode is used to represent that the system is currently in PCV mode, stopVentilation means the user wishes to stop, and VentilationOff is modeled by the atomic proposition StandbyMode (from the mapping description: Standby Mode (same as VentilationOff...)). The LTL template ensures this transition happens immediately after the stop command during PCV mode, matching the specification in the natural language input.

The requirement specifies a state transition: when the system is in PCV mode, the transition to the VentilationOff (StandbyMode) should occur if the user stops ventilation. We have the atomic propositions PCVMode (system is in PCV mode), stopVentilation (user stops the ventilation), and StandbyMode (system is in Standby/VentilationOff mode). The LTL property should state: whenever we are in the PCV mode and the user stops ventilation, then in the next step, we must be in StandbyMode (VentilationOff). This is a classic 'if condition holds, then next state must have...' pattern. Therefore, we choose template 41 (G ((bool_exp1 & bool_exp2) -> (X bool_exp3))). We use bool_exp1 = PCVMode, bool_exp2 = stopVentilation, bool_exp3 = StandbyMode. N_DURATION is not needed in this template.

The property states that whenever the system is in PCVMode and the user requests to stop ventilation (indicated by 'stopVentilation'), a transition to 'StandbyMode' (interpreted as VentilationOff) must happen in the next state. This is captured by the template (11): 'G (bool_exp2 -> (X bool_exp3))', with 'bool_exp2' as 'PCVMode & stopVentilation' and 'bool_exp3' as 'StandbyMode'. This faithfully encodes the required transition because the mode must only switch from PCV to VentilationOff (Standby) when the user actively requests it in PCV mode.

This LTL property formalizes the requirement that, whenever the system is in PCV mode and the user issues a command to stop ventilation ('stopVentilation'), then a transition to the 'VentilationOff' state should occur. Since there is no atomic proposition explicitly called 'VentilationOff', and based on the provided mapping, 'StandbyMode' is described as the same as VentilationOff. Therefore, the LTL property expresses that if both 'PCVMode' is active and 'stopVentilation' is issued, then 'StandbyMode' (i.e., VentilationOff) must hold in the next state. The selected template captures this as a global response: whenever the condition (PCVMode & stopVentilation) becomes true, StandbyMode must immediately follow.

The requirement says: 'The transition from PCV to VentilationOff shall occur if the user stops the ventilation in PCV mode.' We model this by saying that if the system is in PCV Mode (i.e., PCVMode holds) and the user requests to stop ventilation (i.e., stopVentilation holds), then it is required that, in the next step, the system is not ventilating anymore (i.e., ventilating is false). This captures the semantics that stopping ventilation requests a transition to the ventilation-off/standby state. We use template 11, which describes that when a certain condition (here: 'in PCV mode and user stops ventilation') becomes true, another property (here: 'ventilating becomes false in the next step') must hold at the next time instant. N_DURATION is not required for this template and thus left null.

This LTL property expresses that when the system is in PCVMode and the user requests to stop ventilation (stopVentilation is true), there must be a transition to StandbyMode (i.e., VentilationOff). This is expressed as: whenever (PCVMode & stopVentilation) holds, StandbyMode should hold in the next state. This directly captures the requirement that stopping ventilation in PCV mode should transition the system to the ventilation off state. No timing constraint or intermediate state is implied in the input, so the most appropriate pattern is 'if PCVMode and stopVentilation, next StandbyMode'.

The property captures that in PCV mode, if the user requests to stop ventilation (i.e., 'stopVentilation' is true) while ventilation in PCV mode is active (i.e., 'PCVMode' is true), then the ventilation-off mode should be entered (i.e., 'StandbyMode', which corresponds to 'VentilationOff' per the description, is true) in the next time step. This matches the requirement that the system transition from PCV to VentilationOff if the user stops the ventilation while in PCV mode.
